prompt: Design a VetOps Unified Operations Portal to run a multi-location veterinary hospital group entirely on an on‑premise network, covering rentable equipment, medical-supply inventory, internal publishing, and patient-visit service feedback. The Vue.js web interface (English UI) provides role-based workspaces for System Administrator, Clinic Manager, Inventory Clerk, Technician/Doctor, and Content Editor/Approver. Staff can search and manage a rental asset ledger for items like infusion pumps, portable ultrasound units, and oxygen cages, with instant status visibility (available, rented, in maintenance, deactivated), photo/spec previews, and QR/barcode scan lookup using a locally attached scanner. Rental checkout captures renter (department or clinician), expected return time, and deposit/fee terms; the UI blocks double-booking and shows a countdown for overdue returns. Inventory screens support multiple storerooms per facility, with guided flows for receiving, issuing to departments, inter-store transfers, and stocktake entry; low-stock banners appear when on-hand falls below safety stock (default 14 days of average usage) and users can choose reservation strategy per service order (lock inventory at order creation vs. deduct when the order is closed). A content publishing workbench lets editors draft announcements and rotating homepage carousels for staff terminals, route items for approval, target visibility by facility, department, role, and tags, and roll back to a prior version. After a completed visit, staff can hand a local tablet to the pet owner to submit an order-level review with rating, tags, text, and up to 5 images; managers can respond, or trigger an appeal/hide workflow for abusive content while preserving the audit trail, and dashboards summarize average score, negative-review rate, and median response time by clinic and provider.The backend uses Laravel to expose REST-style APIs consumed by the Vue.js client over the local LAN, with MySQL as the system of record for all master data, transactions, and analytics snapshots so the system remains fully functional without internet access. A unified data model and ingestion pipeline standardizes facilities, departments, doctors, services, pricing, business hours, and addresses; bulk CSV import/export is supported with idempotent writes (same external key updates instead of duplicating), row-level validation, and data versioning so every change stores who/when/what with a reversible history. Inventory writes post to an immutable stock ledger that records inbound/outbound/transfer/adjustment, calculates available-to-promise, and enforces variance rules during stocktake (over ±5% requires manager approval and a reason). Rental pricing rules support daily/weekly rates in USD and deposits (default 20% of replacement cost, minimum $50.00), and assets auto-transition to “overdue” after 2 hours past scheduled return. Security is local-only: username/password authentication with minimum 12 characters, salted hashing, configurable inactivity timeout (15 minutes), rate limiting (max 10 login attempts per 10 minutes per workstation) with a built-in CAPTCHA challenge after 5 failures, and full-chain auditing for logins, data edits, exports, approvals, and configuration changes retained for 7 years. The application enforces CSRF protection, output encoding to reduce XSS risk, parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection, and encrypts sensitive fields at rest (for example, masking owner phone numbers to (555) ***‑1234 in non-admin views) while storing all uploaded images and documents on local disk with checksum-based integrity validation. Deduplication and entity resolution run locally during imports and edits using URL normalization for reference links, content fingerprints (SimHash/MinHash) for near-duplicate announcements, and key-field matching for services/providers; merges require manager confirmation and record conflict resolution rules that preserve provenance and change history.
